content.text: CoE Charter, Mandate and Governance Model

A Claude Center of Excellence (CoE) is the organizational unit responsible for scaling AI capability across the enterprise safely and consistently. Without a CoE, Claude adoption fragments: each team invents its own patterns, duplicates effort, creates inconsistent risk exposure, and loses the compounding returns that come from shared infrastructure and shared learning. This sub-subtopic explains what a CoE charter must contain, how to structure its governance, and how to assign decision rights so the CoE operates with speed without bypassing necessary oversight.

A charter is a founding document that defines the CoE's purpose, scope, authority, and accountability. It is not a project plan — it does not list deliverables or timelines. Its job is to answer three questions unambiguously:
The mandate — what problem it solves and what outcome justifies its existence.
What is it authorized to do?
The scope of authority — what decisions it can make vs. which require escalation.
The reporting structure and success metrics — what it must demonstrate to continue receiving investment.

A charter that answers all three questions in one page is more useful than a 20-page policy document. Brevity forces clarity. The hard governance work happens in the RACI and the operating model, not in the charter text.
The mandate is one or two sentences that define what the CoE is responsible for producing. It should be specific enough to exclude things that are not in scope:

Example mandate statements:

NARROW (good for early-stage CoE):
"The Claude CoE is responsible for enabling engineering teams to
ship production Claude integrations safely, by providing reusable
infrastructure, approved patterns, and prompt engineering expertise."

BROAD (appropriate once CoE is established):
"The Claude CoE is responsible for the enterprise's AI capability
— from strategy to delivery — including use case prioritization,
model governance, developer enablement, and risk management for
all Claude deployments."

AVOID (too vague to be useful):
"The Claude CoE is responsible for AI at the company."
The charter must explicitly name what is inside and outside scope. Ambiguity here leads to turf conflicts when a business unit wants to ship a Claude feature without going through the CoE:
Model selection and approved model list
API key provisioning and rotation policy
Product design decisions for Claude-powered features
Hiring decisions for AI roles in business units
Budget allocation for BU-specific AI headcount
Data science or ML projects not using Claude
AI risk policy and classification framework
Effective CoE governance requires four distinct roles with clearly separated responsibilities. Conflating any two of these is the most common governance failure:

The executive sponsor — typically a CTO, CDO, or Chief AI Officer — provides political cover, resolves escalations that cross organizational boundaries, and owns the budget relationship with the board. The sponsor does not run operations; their role is to remove obstacles that the CoE cannot remove at its own level of authority.
Chairs or co-chairs the AI Steering Committee
Signs the CoE charter and any major policy documents
Escalation point for cross-BU disputes about AI priorities
External face of the enterprise's AI program (investor relations, board reporting)

The steering committee sets strategic direction and approves resource allocation. It meets quarterly (or monthly in fast-moving programs) and includes business unit heads, the CIO, legal, security, and finance. Its job is to make decisions that require cross-functional authority — decisions the CoE core team cannot make unilaterally:
Approve the annual AI use case portfolio and budget
Set risk thresholds for Claude use case categories (e.g., what requires legal review)
Arbitrate priority conflicts between business units competing for CoE resources
Review and accept the CoE's quarterly health report
The core team does the work. Typical composition for an enterprise CoE at scale:
Runs the CoE; owns the operating cadence; interface with steering committee
Shared Claude infrastructure: API gateway, logging, rate limiting, SDKs
Prompt standards, review, best practices library, eval frameworks
Threat modeling, data classification, key management, incident response
Portfolio tracking, milestone reporting, resource scheduling
Embedded in each business unit; the CoE's antenna in the business

The RACI above covers the most contested decisions in a CoE's first year. Publish this matrix in the charter and review it every six months — as the CoE matures, more decisions can move from requiring steering committee approval to CoE core authority.
Use Case Intake and Approval Process

One of the CoE's most visible governance functions is approving new Claude use cases. Without a defined intake process, every team implements its own approval path (or bypasses approval entirely). A lightweight intake process:

# Claude Use Case Intake Form

## Basics
- **Use case name:** [Short descriptive title]
- **Business unit:** [Department / team]
- **Requesting team lead:** [Name + email]
- **Target go-live date:** [Quarter / date]

## Description
- **What problem does this solve?** (1–3 sentences)
- **How will Claude be used?** (summarize the interaction pattern)
- **What data will be in prompts?** (categories: public, internal, confidential, PII, PHI)
- **What will Claude's output be used for?** (human review required? automated action?)

## Risk Classification (complete honestly)
- **Will outputs be acted on without human review?** [Yes / No / Sometimes]
- **Could an incorrect output cause financial loss > $10k?** [Yes / No / Unknown]
- **Does this involve PII or PHI?** [Yes / No]
- **Is this customer-facing?** [Yes / No]

## Technical
- **Estimated monthly API spend:** [$X]
- **Engineering team building it:** [Names / team]
- **Expected monthly active users:** [Number]
Based on the risk classification, the CoE routes the request to the appropriate approval level:
Internal use, human review, no PII, <$5k/mo
No human review on outputs OR PHI OR >$50k/mo

The CoE authors and maintains a small set of policy documents that govern Claude use enterprise-wide. These are not the CoE's internal processes — they are external-facing policies that every team using Claude must follow:

What Claude may and may not be used for. Lists prohibited use cases (e.g., autonomous decisions about employee performance, medical diagnosis without oversight) and required practices (e.g., AI disclosure to customers).
Which data categories may be included in Claude prompts, under which conditions. Maps to Anthropic's ZDR and data retention policies.
Which Claude model IDs are approved for use, with the date-qualified versions pinned. Updated when new models are validated.
What constitutes an AI-related security incident, a quality incident, or a compliance incident, and the escalation path for each.

# Acceptable Use Policy — Excerpt

## Prohibited Use Cases
The following uses of Claude are prohibited without explicit exception
approval from the AI Steering Committee:

1. **Autonomous employee evaluation or termination** — Claude outputs
   may inform but never substitute for human HR decisions.

2. **Medical diagnosis or treatment recommendation** — without licensed
   physician review and appropriate regulatory compliance.

3. **Autonomous execution of financial transactions > $1,000** — all
   Claude-generated transaction instructions require human authorization.

4. **Customer communication claiming to be human** — all Claude-generated
   customer communications must be disclosed as AI-generated or AI-assisted
   in the communication itself.

## Required Practices
All teams using Claude must:
- Log all API requests and responses for 90 days
- Never include unredacted PII in prompts without a signed DPA
- Use only model IDs from the CoE Approved Model List
- Report quality incidents to the CoE within 24 hours of discovery
A charter written for a 10-person engineering CoE is wrong for a 50-person enterprise AI function. Build renewal cycles and sunset triggers into the charter itself:
The charter is reviewed by the steering committee every 12 months. Success metrics for the prior year are assessed; scope and authority are adjusted for the next year.
Any of the following triggers an out-of-cycle charter review: a significant AI incident, a major regulatory change (new AI law), an acquisition or divestiture, or a doubling of AI spend.

If the CoE fails to demonstrate its value metrics for two consecutive quarters, the steering committee may vote to dissolve or restructure it. This provision prevents the CoE from becoming a bureaucratic fixture that survives regardless of performance.

The sunset provision is the most important clause in the charter. It is what separates a CoE with real accountability from an organizational unit that exists to justify its own existence. Write it in, and mean it.

The steering committee needs quantitative evidence that the CoE is delivering value. Metrics must be chosen before the CoE launches — retroactively chosen metrics are easy to game. Three categories of metrics cover the health of the CoE's mandate:
Track whether the CoE is enabling teams to ship safely and quickly:
<8 weeks from approved intake to production
95% of reviews completed within stated SLA
Track whether the CoE is preventing incidents and maintaining policy compliance:
Use cases with complete policy sign-off
Track whether Claude adoption is delivering measurable business outcomes:
Total API spend / tasks completed with AI assistance
Shows efficiency trajectory as usage scales
% of eligible teams with at least one production use case
Reveals which BUs are lagging and need enablement
Self-reported hours saved per week × headcount
Qualitative business case for continued investment
Count of teams reusing shared CoE infrastructure vs. building their own

Many enterprises attempt to launch a CoE by writing the charter and org chart first, then hiring into it. This frequently produces an expensive governance function that lacks credibility because it has never shipped anything. The more reliable path:
Start with one high-visibility use case.

Pick a use case that a respected team wants to build and that has medium risk (not trivial, not dangerous). Build it well, document the decisions, and share them broadly. The CoE's early credibility comes from demonstrating it can help teams ship — not from writing policies.

After the first two or three use cases, the repeating patterns become visible: the same intake questions, the same infrastructure setup, the same prompt review concerns. Extract these into reusable standards. This is the first CoE artifact that has practical value.

Write the charter to codify what the CoE is already doing, not to describe an aspirational future state. A charter for a function that has shipped five use cases is credible. A charter for a function that has shipped nothing is a document of intent.
Expand scope as authority is earned.

In the first six months, the CoE should control only what it can demonstrably manage well — probably API key provisioning and shared infrastructure. Scope expands as the organization develops trust in the CoE's judgment.
The charter is not the CoE. The CoE is the people, the relationships, and the delivery track record. The charter formalizes what already works.
Three patterns reliably produce CoE failures, regardless of how well the charter is written:

If every Claude use case must pass through a single team's review before it can proceed, and that team is under-resourced, the CoE becomes an obstacle. Teams route around it, creating shadow AI programs that lack oversight. Fix: tiered approval with clear criteria for self-service vs. review-required paths.
The CoE as a standards body without enforcement.
Publishing a prompt engineering guide that no one is required to follow produces a library that is never read. Fix: make standards a prerequisite for API key provisioning, not a voluntary resource.
The CoE without a business owner.

A CoE run entirely by the technology organization loses sight of the business problems Claude should solve. Without a business owner in the governance structure, the CoE optimizes for technical elegance rather than business impact. Fix: require at least one business unit head on the steering committee, and measure CoE success on business outcomes, not technical outputs.

The steering committee needs quantitative evidence that the CoE is delivering value. Metrics must be chosen before the CoE launches — retroactively chosen metrics are easy to game. Three categories of metrics cover the health of the CoE's mandate:
Track whether the CoE is enabling teams to ship safely and quickly:
<8 weeks from approved intake to production
95% of reviews completed within stated SLA
Track whether the CoE is preventing incidents and maintaining policy compliance:
Use cases with complete policy sign-off
Track whether Claude adoption is delivering measurable business outcomes:
Total API spend / tasks completed with AI assistance
Shows efficiency trajectory as usage scales
% of eligible teams with at least one production use case
Reveals which BUs are lagging and need enablement
Self-reported hours saved per week × headcount
Qualitative business case for continued investment
Count of teams reusing shared CoE infrastructure vs. building their own

Many enterprises attempt to launch a CoE by writing the charter and org chart first, then hiring into it. This frequently produces an expensive governance function that lacks credibility because it has never shipped anything. The more reliable path:
Start with one high-visibility use case.

Pick a use case that a respected team wants to build and that has medium risk (not trivial, not dangerous). Build it well, document the decisions, and share them broadly. The CoE's early credibility comes from demonstrating it can help teams ship — not from writing policies.

Write the charter to codify what the CoE is already doing, not to describe an aspirational future state. A charter for a function that has shipped five use cases is credible. A charter for a function that has shipped nothing is a document of intent.
Expand scope as authority is earned.

In the first six months, the CoE should control only what it can demonstrably manage well — probably API key provisioning and shared infrastructure. Scope expands as the organization develops trust in the CoE's judgment.
The charter is not the CoE. The CoE is the people, the relationships, and the delivery track record. The charter formalizes what already works.
Three patterns reliably produce CoE failures, regardless of how well the charter is written:

If every Claude use case must pass through a single team's review before it can proceed, and that team is under-resourced, the CoE becomes an obstacle. Teams route around it, creating shadow AI programs that lack oversight. Fix: tiered approval with clear criteria for self-service vs. review-required paths.
The CoE as a standards body without enforcement.
Publishing a prompt engineering guide that no one is required to follow produces a library that is never read. Fix: make standards a prerequisite for API key provisioning, not a voluntary resource.
The CoE without a business owner.

A CoE run entirely by the technology organization loses sight of the business problems Claude should solve. Without a business owner in the governance structure, the CoE optimizes for technical elegance rather than business impact. Fix: require at least one business unit head on the steering committee, and measure CoE success on business outcomes, not technical outputs.

A CoE's charter, governance structure, and metrics should evolve as the organization's AI maturity grows. What is appropriate governance for an organization with 5 Claude use cases is over-engineered for 500 and under-engineered for none:
Intake process, first standards, first shared infra
RACI established, policies documented, BU liaisons placed
Self-service portal, automated compliance checks, portfolio mgmt
Adoption rate by BU, cost per task
Federated model with strong central standards, audit function

Each phase transition requires revisiting the charter. The scope authority granted at the Seed phase is too narrow for the Scaling phase — the CoE needs broader autonomy to operate efficiently at scale. Conversely, governance structures designed for 100 use cases would paralyze a seed-phase CoE that needs to move fast to establish credibility.

The most common organizational question as a CoE matures is whether to remain centralized or federate. In a federated model, each major business unit has its own AI capability (the 'spokes'), with the central CoE providing standards, shared infrastructure, and audit (the 'hub'). Criteria for considering federation:
BUs with more than 50 employees using Claude regularly typically benefit from an embedded AI resource rather than relying entirely on the central CoE.
BUs with highly specialized AI use cases (e.g., a clinical AI team in a hospital system) often develop domain expertise that the central CoE cannot match.
If a BU's roadmap requires shipping AI features faster than the central CoE can support, federation with clear standards is better than a bottleneck.

Federation is not a relaxation of standards — it is a redistribution of execution responsibility. The central CoE retains standards-setting, audit, and shared infrastructure even in a fully federated model.

A Claude CoE charter defines mandate (what the CoE exists to produce), scope boundaries (what is inside vs. outside its authority), and governance structure (executive sponsor, steering committee, core team, and liaisons with clear RACI). The charter maintains four policy instruments — Acceptable Use Policy, Data Classification Policy, Approved Model List, and Incident Classification Matrix — that govern all enterprise Claude usage. A lightweight use case intake process with risk-tiered approval levels ensures new deployments are reviewed proportionally to their risk, without creating bottlenecks for low-risk internal tools. Build annual review and sunset provisions into the charter from the start to maintain genuine accountability.
